segments.s180-g161: بدیشان is a fusion of به + ایشان meaning "to/for them" and functions as the indirect object. فراوان is an adverb here modifying the verb (placed before the verb, as is common in classical Persian). بکرد is the past tense verb (ب- prefix + کرد) meaning "he did/uttered," and آفرین is the acclamation/word uttered (an object or quoted exclamation) — together the phrase means "he gave/uttered abundant praise to them."
segments.s180-g162: که here introduces the content of what was said (a complementizer 'that'). آباد is a predicate adjective "prosperous/inhabited," and بادا is the optative particle meaning "may it be" (classical subjunctive/wish). به + گردان زمین is a prepositional phrase: به (to/for) plus گردان ("revolving/round," a modifier of زمین) and زمین ("earth/world"); together: "may the round earth be prosperous."
segments.s180-g121: بپیمودم is a past first‑person verb meaning 'I traversed/rode' (preterite). این بوم ایران is a noun phrase in which بوم ('land, homeland') is specified by ایران ('of Iran') and preceded by the demonstrative این ('this'). بر اسپ is a prepositional phrase with بر ('on, upon') followed by اسپ ('steed/horse') indicating the manner/vehicle of traversal.
segments.s180-g122: ازین is a fused 'از + این' meaning 'from this'; مرز is the boundary 'border'. تا marks the endpoint 'as far as/to' and introduces خان آذرگشسپ, where خان is a title ('khan' = lord/archive/inn depending on context) attached to the proper name آذرگشسپ. Together the phrase gives the extent of the speaker's journey.
segments.s180-g123: ندیدم is the negative past: 'I did not see' (literal 'saw-not I'). کسی را is the indefinite 'someone' marked by the object/definite marker را; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. دلشاد بود is the predicate of that relative clause: دلشاد ('cheerful, content') + بود (past copula 'was').
segments.s180-g124: توانگر modifies the omitted referent ('who was wealthy'). بد here is an archaic/poetic form: in context it is functioning as an elided form of بود ('was') to satisfy meter, so the clause reads effectively 'توانگر بود و بومش آباد بود' — 'was wealthy and his land was prosperous'. و links the two predicates; بومش ('his homeland') + آباد ('prosperous') is finished by the copula بود. Because بد is being used poetically as a contracted copula, it does not carry the ordinary lexical meaning 'bad' here.
segments.s180-g125: همه is 'all/everyone'. خستگانند is a present/stateive plural: 'they are weary/exhausted' (خستگان = the weary, -ند 3pl copula). از introduces the cause/agent 'because of/from', followed by the proper name افراسیاب (Afrasiab).
segments.s180-g126: همه دل is 'all hearts' (همه modifying دل). پر از خون is a descriptive construction where پر ('full (of)') + از + خون ('blood') = 'full of blood' (here metaphorically 'full of blood/blood‑shed, i.e. wounded or grief‑filled'). و links the next clause, دیده پرآب is a compound where دیده ('eye') takes the adjectival compound پرآب ('full of water' = teary). Note the adjective follows the noun as is normal in Persian.
segments.s180-g127: نخستین ('first, foremost') modifies the compound جگرخسته ('heart‑weary, grief‑worn'). از وی = 'because of him / from him' (وی = 'he/that one'). منم is the emphatic copula 'I am' (من + enclitic ـم). The phrase places the speaker as the principal of the grieving.
segments.s180-g128: که introduces a clause explaining/expanding the preceding statement ('who/that'); پر درد is 'full of pain' (پر = 'full (of)', درد = 'sorrow/affliction'); ازویست is an archaic fused expression meaning 'it is from him / due to him' (از وی است → ازویست). جان و تنم lists the possessed items 'my life and my body' (the possessive -م attaches to the last noun, covering both). The clause thus says the speaker's soul and body are filled with suffering attributable to that person.
segments.s180-g153: تن و جان ما is a coordinated subject 'our body and soul' (ما as possessive/subject). سربه‌سر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/wholly'. پیش تست literally 'is before you' (پیش = in the presence of/before), where تست is the archaic/poetic contraction of 'توست' (‘is yours’), so the clause means 'are entirely at your (disposal)'.
segments.s180-g154: غم و شادمانی is a noun pair 'sorrow and joy' presented as what pertains to the addressee. کم و بیش is an idiom 'less and more' = 'more or less'. تست again is the poetic 'توست' ('is yours'), so the clause means 'sorrow and joy, more or less, are yours.'
segments.s180-g155: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from'. The phrase places source first: 'from (the) mother'. همه quantifies the subject 'all'. مرگ را uses the object marker را with 'death' to indicate the fate/target; زاده‌ایم is first-person plural perfect/passive 'we were born' (we have been born), so the structure means 'we are all born from the mother unto death.'
segments.s180-g156: همه is 'all/everyone' as subject. بنده‌ایم is 'we are servants' (بنده + present/affirmative copula -ایم). ارچه is an archaic conjunction meaning 'although/even if'. آزاده‌ایم repeats the first-person plural copula with 'آزاده' ('free' or 'noble-hearted'), so 'we are all servants, although we are free.'
segments.s180-g157: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پاسخ چنین is 'such an answer' (پاسخ = answer, چنین = such/thus). یافت is the past of یاب/یافت 'found/received'. از پیلتن is the source phrase 'from Piltan'. Word order follows a typical poetic SOV structure with the verb یافت near the end.
segments.s180-g158: A simple enumerative prepositional phrase: repeated ز/از 'from' introduces the sources. طوس and گودرز are proper names (here طوس refers to the hero Tous, not the city), و is the conjunction 'and', and انجمن is 'assembly/meeting' — 'from Tous and from Goudarz and from the assembly.'
segments.s180-g159: رخ شاه is a noun‑noun genitive 'the king's face' ( Persian often places possessor after possessee). شد is the past 'became'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by گل ارغوان 'a purple/crimson flower' — the clause says 'the king's face became like a crimson flower.'
segments.s180-g160: که here introduces the reason 'for/because'. دولت is 'the state/sovereignty/fortune' (meaning the realm or its prosperity), جوان is the adjective 'young', بود is the past copula 'was'. و links the coordinated clause with خسرو جوان (Khosrow young), where the verb 'was' is understood to apply to Khosrow as well. So: 'for the realm was young and Khosrow was young.'
segments.s180-g81: که introduces this reported clause (‘that’). هرگز is a sentential negator meaning “never.” نپیچم is negative + present-subjunctive/optative 1st-singular of پیچیدن — here functioning as a jussive/prohibitive (“I will not / may I not turn”). سوی is a preposition placed before its object and governs مهر اوی (‘toward his love’), where اوی is the archaic possessive/enclitic meaning “his.”
segments.s180-g82: نبینم is likewise negative + present-subjunctive/optative 1st-singular of دیدن and parallels نپیچم (‘nor do I see’ / ‘may I not see’). بخواب here indicates the state ‘in sleep’ (i.e. ‘while asleep’). اندرون چهر اوی is a genitive phrase ‘the face of him within (the) inner place,’ with اندرون = ‘inside/within’ and اوی again the possessive enclitic.
segments.s180-g83: یکی is an indefinite subject here (‘someone’ / ‘a certain one’). خط is the direct object meaning a line/writing. بنوشت is an archaic/past form ‘wrote’ (subject–object–verb order is common in poetic Persian); note the listed lemma senses do not include the past-verb meaning, so the appropriate sense is not supplied in the given choices.
segments.s180-g84: به مشکاب is an instrumental/means phrase ‘with (the) musk-water’ (به here functions like ‘by/with’). بر دفتر is a locative/on-phrase ‘on the book,’ and خسروی is an adjectival/attributive meaning ‘royal/of the king’ modifying دفتر.
segments.s180-g85: گوا here functions as ‘witness’/‘witnessed’ and is followed by بود (past ‘was/were’). The personal names (دستان و رستم) follow the verb — a word order frequent in poetry — giving ‘Dastan and Rostam were witnesses.’ برین is a short locative/relational particle (‘to/on this’), written as بر + این in classical style.
segments.s180-g86: بزرگان لشکر is a noun phrase ‘the great ones/ chiefs of the army.’ همه emphasizes ‘all/every one,’ and همچنین is an adverb meaning ‘likewise/also,’ indicating these men shared the same status (they too were witnesses).
segments.s180-g87: به زنهار is a prepositional phrase ‘as a warning / by way of warning’ with به functioning as an instrumental/relational preposition. بر دست رستم is the goal phrase ‘into Rostam’s hand’ (بر + noun = ‘upon/into the hand of…’), and نهاد is the past tense of نهادن meaning ‘placed’ (third-person past).
segments.s180-g88: چنان modifies the following items as ‘such/so’: چنان خط و سوگند و آن رسم و داد lists the things placed — ‘that writing and oath, and that custom and justice/law.’ The coordination with و links parallel nouns (خط = writing/hand, سوگند = oath, رسم = custom, داد = law/justice).
segments.s180-g145: چه at the beginning is an interrogative 'what'. گویید is a 2nd‑person plural/formal present-subjunctive or exhortative form of 'to say' used here as 'do you say'/'you say'. و links the two clauses. این را uses را as the definite object marker marking 'this' as the object. چه before پاسخ again asks 'what/how', and پاسخ دهید combines پاسخ (answer) with دهید (2nd‑person plural imperative/present-subjunctive of 'to give') to mean 'give an answer' or 'how will you answer'.
segments.s180-g146: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. یکسره here means 'altogether/unanimously' or 'completely'. رای is 'opinion/decision/vote' and فرخ (adjective) qualifies it as 'auspicious/favorable'; نهید is the archaic 2nd‑person plural present/imperative form of نهادن 'to place/set' meaning 'place/give' (i.e., 'cast/offer a vote/opinion'). The whole clause is an exhortation: 'All together give a favorable decision.'
segments.s180-g147: بدانید is an imperative/plural present of دانستن 'know' (addressing the assembly). کو here is archaic/interrogative meaning roughly 'who/which one' (etymologically similar to 'کوه' usages) and شد is 'became/was' (past of شدن). به links to the following noun: به بد پیشدست literally 'who became preemptive/in the first in evil' — پیشدست 'preemptively/first' modifies the action 'بِد'. The clause asks them to identify who first took the evil initiative.
segments.s180-g148: مکافات بد 'the retribution/punishment of evil' puts مکافات (retribution) as head and بد (evil) as its qualifier; را is the definite object/ topical marker attached to the whole phrase. نشاید is an impersonal/polite negative meaning 'it is not fitting/one should not', and نشست is the noun/verb 'to sit' (here used metaphorically: 'to sit [upon/over/neglect]' or 'to delay/let remain'). Together they mean roughly 'it is not right to let the punishment of evil sit' — i.e., one must not leave evil unpunished or delay retribution.
segments.s180-g149: بزرگان 'the great ones/elders' is the subject. به پاسـخ uses به to mark the relation 'with respect to / for an answer', and پاسخ is 'answer/reply'. بیاراستند is a past 3rd‑person plural form from آراستن with the prefix بی‑ meaning 'they arranged/composed/adorned' — here 'they framed/put together an answer.'
segments.s180-g150: به درد دل is an idiomatic prepositional phrase: به + درد (pain/sorrow) + دل (heart) = 'with heartfelt sorrow' or 'in the pain of heart'. از جای 'from their places' uses از as 'from' and جای as 'place/seat'. برخاستند is the past 3rd‑person plural of برخاستن 'to rise/get up', so the clause means 'they rose from their places, moved in their hearts.'
segments.s180-g151: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory address (classical poetic 'ای' suffixed to که) meaning 'O (you)'. نامدار جهان is a genitive phrase 'famed/renowned of the world' (name-bearing world‑famous one). شادباش is a wish/formula meaning 'be happy/rejoice' — here an exhortation or congratulatory greeting directed to the addressee.
segments.s180-g152: همیشه 'always' modifies the following wish. ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from'; رنج و غم lists two nouns 'suffering and sorrow' joined by و. آزاد is an adjective 'free', and باش is the 2nd‑person singular imperative of بودن 'be', so the clause is an imperative/wish: 'Be always free from suffering and sorrow.'
segments.s180-g25: وز is an archaic/prepositional particle here (classical/poetic) meaning 'from' (not the modern senses listed), followed by آنجا 'there'. سوی is a directional preposition 'toward' and شهر دیگر is the place phrase 'another city'. شدی is the past-tense form of شدن placed at the end (typical Persian verb-final order) meaning 'became/turned/moved' — the verb applies to the preceding directional phrase.
segments.s180-g26: همی before the verb gives a continuous/habitual or emphatic feel (classical marker). با introduces accompaniment/instrumentality ('with'), and the coordinated list می‌و تخت و افسر ('feasts/fruit, the throne, and the crown/helmet') are the things with which he is described. The final شدی is again the past of شدن used here as a light/copular verb meaning 'was furnished/was made' or 'became'.
segments.s180-g27: همی رفت is a past, ongoing/continuous narrative 'he went/kept going'. تا here means 'as far as' (indicating the extent/destination) and introduces the place name آذرابادگان. Word order places the extent-marker تا + place after the motion verb.
segments.s180-g28: ابا او is a textual/orthographic form equivalent to با او ('with him') — a prepositional phrase indicating accompaniment. بزرگان و آزادگان is a coordinated nominative phrase 'the great ones and the freemen'; the verb ('were') is omitted here (typical in Persian narration) and understood from context.
segments.s180-g29: گهی ... و گهی sets up alternating actions ('sometimes ... and sometimes ...'). باده خورد uses خورد (past of خوردن) for 'drank/partook (of wine)', and تاخت اسپ uses تاخت (past of تاختن) with اسپ 'steed' to mean 'the horse galloped'. Subjects are omitted and recovered from context ('he').
segments.s180-g30: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. سوی introduces the direction/toward which he came, and خان before the proper name is an honorific/title ('Khan'); the name آذرگشسپ follows as the addressee/goal. The whole phrase means 'he came to (the presence of) Khan Azargashasp.'
segments.s180-g31: جهان‌آفرین را uses را as the definite-object marker ('praise to the Creator' / 'he praised the Creator'). ستایش is the noun 'praise' and گرفت is past of گرفتن used as a light verb with the noun (a poetic alternative to ستایش کرد), here meaning 'took up/expressed praise'.
segments.s180-g32: به آتشکده در is a locative complex: به + place + در (in/inside) frames movement/position 'into/at the fire‑temple'. نیایش گرفت repeats the same light-verb pattern with the noun 'prayer' (نیایش گرفت = 'he began/entered into prayer'). The doubled گرفت links the two parallel actions (praising and praying).
segments.s180-g105: This is a nominal clause with the preposition + noun phrase marking the domain of the predicate: به + (ویران و آباد) = "on/upon the ruined and the prosperous." نفرین اوست is a copular construction: نفرین (curse) is the subject, اوست = او + ست is the copula ‘he/it is’ attributing the curse to him. Word order is prepositional phrase → predicate, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s180-g106: دل بیگناهان پر از کین اوست is another copular clause: دل بیگناهان (the hearts of the innocent) is the subject phrase, پر از + noun is the adjectival phrase meaning “full of …”, followed by کین (hatred/vengeance) as the complement, and اوست (he/it is) as the copula indicating that this fullness of hatred is because of/against him.
segments.s180-g107: به بیداد is an adverbial prepositional phrase here meaning “by/through injustice.” خون سیاوش is the direct object (blood of Siavash). بریخت is a past action verb (classical/past form of ریختن) meaning “(he) spilled/poured,” so the clause reads “(he) spilled Siavash’s blood by injustice.”
segments.s180-g108: بدین مرز is the locative/adverbial phrase “at/at that border” (بدین = ‘by/at this’ + مرز = border). باران آتش is a metaphoric noun phrase “a rain of fire.” ببخت (spelled ببیخت here) is an older/poetic verbal form meaning that such a rain “befell/occurred” — the verb is in past or stative sense indicating that the rain of fire came upon that frontier.
segments.s180-g109: دل شهریاران is the subject: “the hearts of the kings.” پر از + بیم is the adjectival predication “full of fear.” اوست (he/it is) is the copula tying the fear to him — i.e. the kings’ hearts are full of fear of him. Word order: subject → predicative phrase → copula.
segments.s180-g110: This clause is a verbless/copiular statement where بلا بر زمین تخت و دیهیم is the nominal phrase ‘calamity upon the land, the throne, and the countryside.’ بر is the preposition “upon/on,” and اوست (he/it is) again attributes that calamity to him. The list زمین، تخت و دیهیم names the spheres affected.
segments.s180-g111: به کین پدر is an instrumental/causal phrase “for the vengeance of (my) father.” بنده را is the definite object marked by را (“the servant”), and دست‌گیر here functions as an imperative/request meaning “take by the hand / help” (literally ‘hand‑holder’ or ‘be a helper’). The phrase reads as an appeal: “For my father’s vengeance, help my servant.”
segments.s180-g112: ببخشای is an imperative/optative verb meaning “forgive/spare/grant” (poetic imperative). بر جان + name is a conventional Persian collocation meaning “for the life of X” (i.e., spare X’s life). پیر modifies کاوس as an epithet ‘old/elder’ (Kay Kavus). The overall sense is a plea: “Spare the life of old Kay Kavus.”
segments.s180-g97: تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'until'; برکشید is past tense (3rd singular) 'raised/rose' and the subject آفتاب follows it (poetic inversion). شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'the dark night' that serves as the temporal span governed by تا.
segments.s180-g98: خروشان is an adverbial/participial descriptor ('roaring/flowing') modifying the verbal phrase; همی بود is an archaic progressive/past construction ('was still/kept on being'), with بود the past copula; دیده پرآب is a predicate nominal 'eyes full of water' (i.e. teary eyes).
segments.s180-g99: چنین گفت is the speech-introducing phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus'); کای is the vocative 'O' addressing the addressee; دادگر and یک خدای form the title 'O just/justifying one God' with گفت in past tense introducing direct speech.
segments.s180-g100: A string of epithets in apposition to 'خدای' linked by و: جهاندار (sovereign/lord of the world), روزی‌ده (bestower of sustenance), رهنمای (guide). The conjunction و simply lists attributes of the addressed deity.
segments.s180-g101: به‌روز جوانی is a temporal phrase 'in the day/prime of youth' (poetic genitive-like timing). تو کردی رها is a past clause with explicit subject تو 'you' + کردی (you did) and رها functioning as the participial/complement 'set free'.
segments.s180-g102: مرا is the object ('me') written here in two pieces (م + را); بی‌سپاه is a postpositive description 'without an army/defenceless' that belongs to مرا; از دم اژدها is a prepositional phrase 'from the dragon's breath' (i.e. from the dragon's attack).
segments.s180-g103: تو دانی که introduces a subordinate clause with دانی 'you know that'; سالار توران سپاه is a compact, poetic ordering meaning 'the commander (سالار) of Turan's army/host' (possession and noun order are compressed for meter).
segments.s180-g104: نه ... نه ... is correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'; داند is the present/epic present meaning 'they/him/it knows/possess' governing both nouns; here the phrase says they have neither prudence (پرهیز) nor shame (شرم) in regard to sin (گناه).
segments.s180-g1: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The clause has subject خورشید (the sun) followed by the object تیغ and the prepositional phrase از میان ('from the midst'); Persian poetry often places the finite verb برکشید (past, 3rd singular) at the end. برکشید here is the perfective past meaning 'drew/raised (a weapon)'.
segments.s180-g2: This is the main clause: شب (night) + predicate adjective تیره (dark) + past verb گشت ('became/turned') followed by the prepositional phrase از جهان ('from the world') and the adjective ناپدید ('vanished'). گشت is the simple past 3rd singular used as a copular/auxiliary verb; ناپدید functions as the resulting state 'vanished'.
segments.s180-g3: تبیره is a noun ('explanation/proclamation') and is the grammatical subject; برآمد is past 3rd singular meaning 'arose/issued forth'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and درگاه شاه is the genitive phrase 'the king's court/presence'. The clause means a proclamation came out of the royal court.
segments.s180-g4: This clause has the adverbial phrase به سر ('onto the head(s)') first, then the past plural verb برنهادند ('they raised'), followed by the subject گردان ('the troop/troops') and the object کلاه ('hats/helmets'). Poetic word order inverts normal prose order, but meaning is 'the troop raised their helmets onto their heads.'
segments.s180-g5: چو here functions as the narrative 'when' introducing a list of people. Proper names (طوس, گودرز, گیو) are coordinated with و, and the adjective دلیر follows and modifies گیو ('Giv the brave') — Persian adjectives typically follow the noun. The clause lists actors who appear in the scene.
segments.s180-g6: Again چو = 'when'. This is another coordinated group of names joined by و. گرگین is used as a proper name here; گستهم is likewise a personal name in context. بهرام is followed by شیر as an appositive/epithet ('Bahram the lion'). The verb that completes the clause is omitted here because the poet continues the enumeration.
segments.s180-g7: گرانمایگان ('the illustrious ones') is the plural subject, followed by the locative/prepositional phrase نزد شاه ('in the presence of the king') and the past plural verb آمدند ('they came'). نزد here means 'before/in the presence of' rather than mere proximity.
segments.s180-g8: بران is an adverbial/locative meaning roughly 'to/thereupon' and modifies آمدند ('they came'), followed by the compound noun نامور بارگاه ('renowned court/palace') — note the adjective نامور precedes the noun here by poetic inversion. The verb آمدند repeats to emphasize their arrival at that celebrated court.
segments.s180-g17: هران is an older fused form meaning 'each (of them)'; it quantifies the following nouns. بوم means 'homeland/land' and the coordinating و simply links the next noun. برکان here is a place/region word whose exact sense is dialectal/poetic (not one of the provided senses), and آباد is an adjective 'prosperous/inhabited'. بود is the past copula 'was' (classical past). The phrase order is quantifier + nouns + predicate adjective + past copula, i.e. 'each land ... was prosperous'.
segments.s180-g18: This line repeats the copula for emphasis: تبه بود ('it was bad/ill') and ویران ('ruined') followed by ز + بیداد + بود to state the cause. ز here functions as 'because of/from' and بیداد means 'injustice/oppression'. Each بود is the past copula, so the structure is 'it was X and (it) was Y because of Z'.
segments.s180-g19: درم داد here is literal: درم 'dirham/money' + داد as the past verb 'gave' (not the abstract 'justice'). The conjunction و links the next verb phrase آباد کردش: آباد is an adjective 'prosperous/inhabited' and کردش is the past of کردن 'to make/do' with the enclitic -ش 'it' (object), so 'he made it prosperous'. ز گنج = 'from the treasure' (z = 'from').
segments.s180-g20: This parallel phrase uses z 'from' twice: ز داد 'from justice' (here داد is the abstract noun 'justice') and ز بخشش 'and from giving/largesse'. نیامدش is the negative past 'did not come' with enclitic -ش 'to it', so نیامدش رنج = 'suffering did not come to it' (i.e. it suffered no hardship).
segments.s180-g21: به هر شهر بنشست is 'he sat/established himself in each city'—به marks direction/place, هر is 'every', شهر 'city', and بنشست is the past of بنشستن 'to sit/settle'. و links the second coordinated verb clause بنهاد تخت, where بنهاد is the archaic past of نهادن 'to put/establish' and تخت is 'throne' (object). The order is prepositional phrase + verb, then conjunction + verb + object.
segments.s180-g22: چنانچون is a comparative 'just as/like', followed by بود (past copula) and the noun phrase خسرو نیک‌بخت 'the fortunate king'. Here خسرو functions as 'king/sovereign' (title) and نیک‌بخت is an epithet meaning 'fortunate'. The clause comments that his behavior matched that of the fortunate king.
segments.s180-g23: همه is a general quantifier 'all/every' that scopes over the listed items (بدره 'poetic beauty/full-moon image' and جام 'cup'). The verb می‌خواستی is the imperfect/past-habitual 'you desired/you would wish', so the line lists things one could desire. The order is quantifier + items + verb, common in poetic enumeration.
segments.s180-g24: به here marks instrument/means 'with/by' (classical usage). دینار = 'dinar/money' is the instrument, گیتی 'the world' is the object, and بیاراستی is an older past form 'you adorned' (the -ی ending marks 2nd person past in classical Persian), so the whole means 'you adorned the world with dinars'. The provided dictionary senses for بیاراستی do not match its verbal use here.
segments.s180-g65: A short enumerative phrase: the preposition به is repeated before each item to mark them as things being considered (here: what might tempt someone). The conjunction و simply links the nouns. In this context each noun names a symbol of rank or worldly reward.
segments.s180-g66: This is the main clause: به گفتار = 'by/through words' (به used as an instrument/circumstance). با او = 'with him' / 'from him' (refers back to the speaker or to that speech); نگردی is a negative subjunctive/imperative of گشتن meaning 'do not turn / be turned' directed at the addressee; ز راه = 'from the way/path' (ز = 'from', راه = figurative 'way').
segments.s180-g67: بگویم is 1st-person present-subjunctive/deliberative ('shall I say' / 'let me tell'); که introduces the subordinate question/indirect clause; بنیاد سوگند چیست is an inverted interrogative: 'what is the foundation of an oath' with چی ست (چی = what, ست = copula 'is').
segments.s180-g68: Fronted topical objects: خرد را ('for/w.r.t. wisdom' with را marking it as a definite/ topical object) و جان ترا ('and your life' where ت is the 2nd-person suffix on جان and the following را is the object/topic marker). پند چیست is 'what counsel/advice is there'. Word order places the topics before the predicate.
segments.s180-g69: بگویی is 2nd-person subjunctive/optative ('you might say' / 'you may utter'); به before دادار, خورشید, ماه functions as an oath formula 'by ...'; دادار = the Creator; خورشید و ماه = sun and moon linked by و.
segments.s180-g70: Another enumerative string introduced by به (here marking the thing sworn by: 'by the sword, by love, by the throne, and (by) the cap'). Each به+noun pair is parallel; مهر here is used in the sense 'affection / love' and تخت, کلاه continue the list of worldly/emblematic items.
segments.s180-g71: Parallel oath-phrases: به فر = 'by the farr' (the divine royal glory), و به نیک‌اختر ایزدی = 'and by Nīk-ākhtar the divine' — به here is the oath-preposition 'by'; نیک‌اختر is treated as an epithet/proper name and ایزدی qualifies it as divine.
segments.s180-g72: که introduces the following admonition clause; هرگز = 'never'; نپیچی is negative subjunctive/imperative of پیچیدن 'do not turn'; به سوی = a double prepositional phrase meaning 'toward' (به here 'towards/into', سوی 'toward(s)'); بدی = 'evil'.
segments.s180-g129: دگر precedes the clause and here marks a change/contrast (literally 'again' / 'no longer' in context); چون + نیا is a simile/comparative 'like the ancestors' (نیا = 'ancient/ancestral'); شاه 'king' and آزادمرد 'noble/generous man' together form the noun phrase describing the person being compared. Word order is: contrastive particle → comparative marker → noun ('دگر چون نیا شاه آزادمرد').
segments.s180-g130: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause modifying the preceding noun phrase; از دل 'from the heart' uses the preposition از + noun دل to mark source/inner origin; همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle marking habitual or ongoing action; برکشد is the verb (present/subjunctive form ‘draws up / lifts’) with the object following; باد سرد is the noun phrase 'cold wind' (باد = wind, سرد = cold) that the verb acts upon.
segments.s180-g131: به ایران introduces the location/scene ('in/at Iran'); the coordination زن و مرد lists 'women and men' (و is the conjunction); ازو 'because of/from him' links the cry to that person; با خروش 'with an outcry' is an adverbial phrase (با = 'with', خروش = 'cry/uproar'). The order is locative → people → source/agent → manner.
segments.s180-g132: ز بس is a poetic/concessive prepositional phrase here meaning 'from so much' or 'because of so great an amount'; what follows is a paratactic list of nouns (verbal nouns allowed): کشتن 'killing', غارت 'plunder', جنگ 'war', جوش 'surge/uproar' joined by و 'and'. The initial ز governs the whole list and indicates cause/reason.
segments.s180-g133: کنون‌گر combines کنون 'now' with the conditional particle گر (here fused) and functions as 'if now...'; همه is the subject 'all/everyone'; ویژه یار is a nominal phrase 'chosen/special companions' (ویژه modifies یار); منید is the 2nd-person-plural copular form: من (root for 'my') + the clitic/ending ید indicating 'you (pl.) are' — in practice this yields 'you are mine' or 'you are my ...'.
segments.s180-g134: بدل here means 'in return' or 'as a recompense'; سربسر is an adverbial intensifier 'altogether/entirely/everywhere' modifying the following predicate; دوستدار is a noun 'supporter/admirer' (literally 'one who holds affection'); منید again contains من 'my' + the verbal ending ید (2pl copula) — the whole phrase is an exhortation 'be wholly my supporters' addressed to the same 'you'.
segments.s180-g135: به کین پدر is a prepositional phrase 'for (the) vengeance of (my) father' (به = for/toward, کین = vengeance, پدر = father). بست is a participial/past form 'bound/pledged' (here implying 'having sworn'); خواهم is the 1st-person auxiliary 'I will / I desire' used to mark future/intent; میان is the adverb/prepositional adverb 'among / in the midst'. Together they set up 'having pledged for my father's vengeance, I will (go) among (them)...' with بست describing the state motivating the action.
segments.s180-g136: بگردانم is a first-person-singular present/subjunctive form with the prefix بـ (functioning here like a subjunctive/intent form) meaning 'I will turn / cause to turn / remove'; این بد means 'this evil' (این = this, بد = bad/evil); ز ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'from the Iranians' (ز = from, ایرانیان = Iranians) that marks the source from which the evil will be removed. The verb therefore takes این بد as its object and ز ایرانیان as the source/beneficiary phrase.
segments.s180-g73: میانجی (here spelled می‌انجی) functions as 'mediator' or 'intermediary' (poetic noun). نخواهی is the 2nd-person negative/future-form (you will not / you will have no), and جز از ... marks the sole exception; the preposition از introduces the instruments تیغ و گرز ('the sword and the mace') as the only mediators or recourse. Word order is emphatic: noun + negative verb + exception phrase.
segments.s180-g74: منش برز داری is a simple present construction: منش 'character/nature' + برز 'exalted/high' as an attributive phrase, and داری is the 2nd-person present of داشتن 'to have' (you have/possess). The following و joins a further predicate بالای برز, where بالای is a comparative/positional adjective 'higher/superior' (literally 'above') modifying the repeated noun برز to intensify the exalted rank.
segments.s180-g75: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past 3rd-singular 'heard'. The prepositional particle ز (here forming زو) is the poetic contraction of از + او/آن meaning 'of it' or 'from it' (i.e. 'heard of it'). شهریار جوان ('the young king') is the subject; the order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect but reads 'When the young king heard of it...'.
segments.s180-g76: سوی is the preposition 'toward(s)'. آتش is the object 'fire'. آورد is past 3rd-singular of آوردن 'bring/turn' used here in the idiom روی آوردن = 'to turn one's face (toward)'; therefore آورد governs the following objects روی و روان ('face and spirit'), a paired accusative expression meaning he directed both his outward countenance and inner disposition toward the fire.
segments.s180-g77: به before an oath means 'by' (marking the object of the oath). دادار دارنده is a double epithet 'the Creator, the Holder/Possessor' in apposition, and سوگند خورد is the fixed verbal phrase 'to take/swear an oath' — سوگند 'oath' + خورد (here a light verb meaning 'took' in this compound). The verb خورد is an archaic/light use forming the collocation rather than its literal senses.
segments.s180-g78: This is a two-part parallel phrase expanding the oath: به‌روز سپید = 'by the (bright) day' where به‌روز is a poetic locution 'of the day' and سپید qualifies it as 'bright/white'; و links the two halves; شب لاژورد = 'the lapis/azure night' where لاژورد is a color-image 'azure/ultramarine' describing night. The repetition and adjective-noun order are typical of elevated, poetic diction.
segments.s180-g79: A series of oath-objects introduced by repeated به ('by') for rhetorical emphasis. به خورشید و ماه = 'by the sun and the moon'; the second pair به تخت و کلاه = 'by the throne and (head-)crown' (کلاه here naming a head-ornament). The repeated و simply links items in the enumerative series; the syntax is paratactic and typical of solemn oaths.
segments.s180-g80: Continuation of the oath-list with repeated به introducing each item: به مهر here likely invokes the deity/seat 'Mithra' (a proper-name/deity sense) rather than ordinary 'love'; then به تیغ 'by the sword'; and به دیهیم شاه 'by the rustic/village king' (دیهیم as an adjective or epithet). The repetition of به before each noun stresses the solemnity and variety of witnesses invoked.
segments.s180-g113: تو دانی که — تو (you) + دانی (present/past-poetic 2nd/3rd-person form of دانستن here: 'you know') introduces a subordinate clause with که 'that'. او را uses را as the definite/object marker attached to او, marking him as the person possessing the following property; بدی گوهرست is a predicate: بدی 'evil' is presented as his intrinsic quality and گوهرست is the copular/predicative compound ('is (of) essence' / 'is (his) nature') in poetic diction.
segments.s180-g114: همان بدنژادست و افسونگرست — همان ('that' / emphatic 'indeed') picks up the preceding clause and points to the same person; بد‌نژادست is a compound adjective + copula (بدنژاد + -ست) meaning 'is ill-/low-born'; و links the coordinated predicate افسونگرست, another adjective+nominative copula meaning 'is an enchanter' or 'is bewitching'. The copula -ست is the archaic/poetic form of 'is'.
segments.s180-g115: فراوان بمالید رخ بر زمین — فراوان is an adverb 'plentifully/often' modifying the verb. بمالید (مالید) is simple past 3rd‑person 'rubbed' (poetic word-order places the verb before its object). رخ 'face' is the verb's direct object and بر زمین is a prepositional phrase 'onto/upon the ground' indicating the place of the action.
segments.s180-g116: همی خواند بر کردگار آفرین — همی is the classical/historic continuous marker meaning 'kept on / was continually', used with the past verb خواند (خواند here = 'uttered/called out/recited'). بر کردگار is the beneficiary/target 'to the Creator' and آفرین is the exclamation 'praise/acclamation' (i.e. 'Praise be to the Creator'). The order is typical of poetic Persian (adverb + verb + prepositional phrase + object/exclamation).
segments.s180-g117: وزان جایگه شد سوی تخت باز — وزان ('and from that') links back to the preceding episode. جایگه 'place/spot' is the locative being referenced. شد is the past 3rd‑person copula/auxiliary (here functioning as the simple past motion/occurrence marker: 'he went/moved/was moved'), and سوی تخت باز is a prepositional phrase 'toward the throne again' (سوی = 'toward', تخت = 'throne', باز = 'again'). The sequence reflects poetic inversion but reads as 'from that place he again turned toward the throne.'
segments.s180-g118: بر پهلوانان گردن‌فراز — بر + plural noun here functions adverbially/relationally as 'in front of / before / toward' the group named; پهلوانان is 'the champions/heroes' and گردن‌فراز is an attributive compound 'neck‑raising' = 'proud' (i.e. 'the proud champions'). Together the phrase indicates his movement/position relative to those heroes.
segments.s180-g119: چنین گفت کای نامداران من — چنین گفت is the common narrative formula 'thus he said.' کای is the archaic/vocative 'O' used to address listeners, and نامداران من 'my famed ones' is the vocative addressee (نامداران = 'the renowned/illustrious', من here marks possession: 'my'). The line sets up a direct address to named heroes.
segments.s180-g120: جهانگیر و خنجر گزاران من — جهانگیر can be read as a title/name ('Jahangir' / 'world‑conqueror') and is placed first in the vocative list; و links the second term. خنجر گزاران is a compound plural meaning 'those who wield/bring the dagger' (i.e. 'dagger‑bearers' or 'men of the dagger'), with -ان marking plurality; من again indicates possession 'my'. The whole segment is a vocative enumeration of the addressees.
segments.s180-g33: بیامد is a past (3rd-person) verbal form 'came' (poetic/simple past). خرامان is an adverbial/epithet meaning 'with a proud/stately gait' and here follows the verb for poetic effect. ازان جایگاه is a prepositional phrase (از + آن = 'from that') + جایگاه 'place', giving the origin; ازان is an archaic/poetic contraction of از آن.
segments.s180-g34: نهادند is past (3rd-person plural) of نهادن 'to place/set' used here idiomatically as 'inclined/placed' (i.e. they bowed or inclined). سر is the direct object 'head' (physical head) meaning they bent their heads. سوی is a preposition 'toward', and 'کاوس شاه' is the name plus title ('Kay Kavus, the king'). Word order (verb before object) is a poetic inversion.
segments.s180-g35: نشستند here is the past plural of 'نشستن' (they sat) — the provided lemma list seems mistaken, so this sense is the verb 'sat'. هر دو 'both/either' is the subject, به هم 'together' modifies the action, and شادمان is a predicative adjective 'happy/joyful' describing their state while sitting. The clause uses a compact, poetic order (verb then subject phrase) rather than strict modern prose order.
segments.s180-g36: نبودند is the negative past copula 'they were not'. جز 'except' introduces the restriction, so 'نبودند جز شادمان' = 'they were nothing but joyful'. یک زمان lit. 'one time' functions adverbially = 'for a moment' or 'for a short while'.
segments.s180-g37: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. پر شد is the past 'became full' (پر = 'full', شد = 'became'); the poetic word order places پر شد before the subject. سر 'head' here means 'one's head' (metonymically indicating drunkenness/intoxication). از جام روشن‌گلاب is a prepositional phrase 'from the cup of Roshan‑Golab' (روشن‌گلاب is a poetic epithet for the drink or cup, 'bright/rosy‑scented').
segments.s180-g38: به خواب و به آسایش are prepositional/adverbial phrases 'into sleep and into repose/rest'. آمد is past 'came'. شتاب (literally 'haste') is used adverbially after the verb (poetic inversion of 'شتاب آمد') meaning 'he quickly fell/was brought' into sleep and rest — i.e. he fell asleep swiftly.
segments.s180-g39: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. روز درخشان is the noun phrase 'the shining/bright day'. برآورد is a poetic past of برآوردن meaning 'raised/brought forth' or 'unveiled/opened' (here 'caused to appear' or 'broke [the night]'), and چاک 'cleft/gash' is the object — an image of the day opening a gap in the darkness. Poetic inversion places verb before object.
segments.s180-g40: بگسترد is past (3rd-person) of گستراندن 'to spread' meaning 'it spread'. یاقوت 'ruby' is used metaphorically for the red light of dawn. بر تیره خاک is a prepositional phrase 'over the dark earth' (تیره = dark, خاک = earth/soil). The clause pictures the dawn's ruby light spreading over the dark ground.
segments.s180-g137: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). همگنان is the subject 'fellows/peers'. رای جنگ آورید is a verbal clause where رای ('counsel/plan') is modified by جنگ ('war') and آورید (2nd-person plural present/subjunctive/imperfective) means 'bring/offer' — here 'propose/decide on'. The whole segment reads 'If the fellows put forward a counsel of war.'
segments.s180-g138: بکوشید is a 2nd-person plural jussive/imperative 'strive/attempt'. و is the coordinating 'and/then'. رسم پلنگ آورید is another jussive clause: رسم ('manner/custom/style') + پلنگ ('leopard') + آورید ('bring/assume'). Literally 'bring the leopard's custom' = 'adopt the leopard-like manner'.
segments.s180-g139: مرا is the object 'me' (here written as م + را). این سخن is the nominal phrase 'this saying/word'. پیش بیرون شود uses پیش ('before/previously') + بیرون شود (passive/impersonal construction with شود: 'be put out/removed' or 'become out') with شود carrying an optative/jussive sense 'may it be'. Together: 'May this saying be removed from me beforehand.'
segments.s180-g140: ز introduces cause/agency ('from/through/because of'). جنگ یلان is a genitive 'the war/battle of the heroes' (جنگ = war, یلان = heroes). کوه هامون places 'کوه' (mountain) before هامون (the plain-name 'Hamun') to express 'Hamun will be turned into a mountain'. شود again is jussive/optative 'may it become'. The clause: 'May the plain of Hamun become a mountain because of the heroes' battle.'
segments.s180-g141: هران خون که آید به کین ریخته is a relative clause: هران ('each of those/whichever') qualifies خون ('blood'). که آید is the relative 'that comes/happens' (here 'that is shed'), and به کین ریخته is a participial phrase 'spilled/poured for vengeance' (ریخته as past participle). Together: 'whichever blood is shed in vengeance.'
segments.s180-g142: گنهکار is a predicate adjective/noun 'guilty' or 'sinner'. او is the third‑person pronoun 'he' referring to the culprit. باشد is jussive/optative ('let him be' / 'shall be'), and آویخته is a past participle 'hung' (i.e. 'be hanged'). The word order (predicate before باشد) is typical in classical Persian: 'Let him be guilty and be hanged.'
segments.s180-g143: وگر is 'if/but if'. کشته گردد is a passive construction: کشته (past participle 'killed/slain') + گردد (subjunctive/optative/passive auxiliary 'be/is made'). کسی is the indefinite 'someone/anyone'. زین سپاه here functions as 'of/from this host/army' — note that in the manuscript z + این often fuse as زین; this token in the provided senses list is primarily the noun 'saddle', so its lexical senses don't match the poetic fused use (see senses mapping). The clause reads 'And if anyone of this host is slain.'
segments.s180-g144: بهشت is 'Paradise'. بلندش is بلند ('high/lofty') with the enclitic ش ('his/its'), so 'his lofty'. بود is the copula 'was/would be' (here conditional/epic past sense), and جایگاه is 'place' or 'station'. The clause literally: 'His place would be lofty in Paradise' = 'his place in Paradise will be exalted.'
segments.s180-g49: بسی is a classical/pre‑posed quantifier meaning 'many' (emphatic). پهلوانان is the plural noun 'heroes/warriors'. که introduces a relative clause ('who/that...') and links to the predicate بیجان شدند, where بیجان = 'lifeless/dead' and شدند is the past/copular verb 'became/were'. The whole segment literally: 'many heroes who became lifeless.'
segments.s180-g50: زن و کودک خرد is a coordinated noun phrase ('women and small/small‑grown children'); خرد is a post‑nominal adjective meaning 'small/young'. پیچان شدند is a verbal phrase in which پیچان (lit. 'twisting/wrenching') describes the outcome and شدند is the past copula/auxiliary ('became/were'); together it conveys that they were left twisted/helpless/lifeless in the violence.
segments.s180-g51: بسی شهر بینی is an address to the addressee: بسی 'many', شهر 'cities', and بینی is the 2nd‑person present form 'you see' (here functioning like a future/imperative 'you will see'). ز ایران خراب is a prepositional phrase 'ruined of/from Iran' = 'cities of Iran ruined.'
segments.s180-g52: تبه گشته is a verbal/adjectival phrase: تبه here conveys 'ruin/mischief' and گشته is the participial/perfective 'has become / is become' → 'has become ruined'. از رنج افراسیاب is an ablative phrase 'because of / from the suffering/cruelty of Afrasiab' indicating the cause.
segments.s180-g53: ترا (here split as ت + را) is the dative/beneficiary 'to you'. ایزدی is an adjective 'divine' modifying the following item. هرچ means 'whatever' or 'whatever thing', and بایدت is باید ('it is necessary / ought') with the enclitic ‑ت marking second person—'is necessary for you'. هست is the copula 'is'. The clause literally: 'To you, divine, whatever is necessary for you exists/ be (granted).'
segments.s180-g54: This is a list of sources/means: ز بالا 'from on high', و از دانش 'and from knowledge', و زور دست 'and from the strength/power of the hand'. ز/از are poetic prepositions 'from'; دست here carries the sense 'power/control' rather than merely 'hand'. The series specifies where the needed things should come from.
segments.s180-g55: ز introduces the means or source: 'by/through'. فر تمامی is 'complete/entire farr' (farr = royal glory/splendour). و links to نیک‌اختری, a compound meaning 'good‑starredness' or 'auspicious fortune' (not here a proper name but the quality of being well‑starred). Together they name the blessings or qualities granting superiority.
segments.s180-g56: ز شاهان means 'over/from the kings' (i.e. with respect to other kings). به هر گونه‌ای is an adverbial phrase 'in every kind/manner', where گونه‌ای means 'manner/way'. برتری is the noun 'superiority/preeminence' and functions as the outcome: 'you have superiority over kings in every way.'
segments.s180-g41: جهاندار بنشست is a simple past clause: جهاندار (a title, 'the sovereign') is the subject and بنشست is the 3rd-person singular past of نشستن 'to sit down'. The coordinating و links this clause to the following noun phrase; کاوس کی uses کی as a relative/introducing particle 'who', attaching the upcoming descriptive phrase to Kaavus.
segments.s180-g42: دو شاه سرافراز و دو نیک‌پی is a nominal description: دو (the numeral 'two') precedes شاه ('king') and is followed by the adjective/epithet سرافراز ('lofty, triumphant'). The coordinating و links the second coordinated noun phrase دو نیک‌پی ('two of good birth / well-born'); together this completes the relative begun by کی (i.e. 'who had two proud kings and two well-born [men]').
segments.s180-g43: This segment lists participants and sets them as speaking together: the sequence (ابا رستم گرد و دستان) is a coordinated series of names/titles, followed by the adverbial به هم ('together'). In classical Persian the names can appear in a row without additional particles; به هم here modifies the upcoming verb (they acted/speaking together).
segments.s180-g44: همی گفت is a common classical/perfective construction: همی is an older verbal prefix/adverb marking continuity or habitual aspect with verbs, here combined with گفت (past 'said') to mean 'spoke/said (together)'. کاوس is the indirect object/recipient ('to Kaavus') and the phrase هر بیش و کم is an idiomatic accusative/obj phrase meaning 'everything, both more and less' (i.e. 'great and small'), with هر 'every/each' qualifying بیش 'more' and کم 'less'.
segments.s180-g45: از افراسیاب اندر آمد نخست is a fronted locative/agent phrase plus verb: از before a proper name here introduces the person (literary usage), افراسیاب is the name, اندر آمد is the two-word verb 'to come in / enter' (اندر = in, آمد = came), and نخست is an adverb 'first' placed at the end to mark sequence.
segments.s180-g46: دو رخ را به خون دو دیده بشست is an object-verb clause: دو رخ را = 'the two faces' with را as the definite/direct-object marker; به خون here uses به as instrumental/with ('with blood'); دو دیده = 'the two eyes' and بشست is the 3rd-person past 'he washed'. Classical Persian permits multiple objects before the verb, so the sequence means he 'washed the two faces and the two eyes with blood.'
segments.s180-g47: بگفت is the past third-person 'he said' (بـ- is a prefixed form), and آنکه introduces the reported clause (literally 'he who' / 'the one who'); the subordinate clause او با سیاوش چه کرد follows normal classical SOV order: او (he) + با سیاوش ('with Siyâvash') + چه ('what') + کرد (did), so the whole means 'he who recounted/said what he did with Siyâvash.'
segments.s180-g48: از ایران سراسر is an adverbial phrase 'from all of Iran / throughout Iran' (از + noun + سراسر 'throughout'). برآورد is the past of بر آوردن and here means 'brought forth / raised / gathered' (in older epic usage it often means 'mustered' or 'brought together'), and the final گرد is an archaic verbal/auxiliary element that occurs in compounds (here completing the verbal phrase: 'he gathered/raised'). The phrase thus says he 'gathered/raised [forces] from all over Iran.'
segments.s180-g89: ازان پس is an adverbial temporal phrase 'after that/thereupon'. همی is the classical/progressive marker meaning 'kept on' or 'continually' and precedes the verbal idea. خوان (banquet/feasting) is coordinated with the following verb by و; می‌خواستند is past‑tense 3rd plural 'they wanted/sought' (here: asked for wine). Poetic word order places the time phrase and the continuous marker up front and coordinates noun and verb more loosely than in colloquial Persian.
segments.s180-g90: ز هر گونه مجلس is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'of/from every kind of assembly' (ز + determiner هر + noun گونه + head مجلس). بیاراستند is a past 3rd plural verb 'they adorned/embellished.' The preposition ز here marks the domain ('of every sort') and the verb follows the entire noun phrase as typical in classical word order.
segments.s180-g91: ببودند is an archaic/poetic past 3rd plural 'they were / they remained' (the initial ب is a poetic prefix). The time expression یک هفته ('for a week') follows the verb by inversion typical of poetry. با رود و می is a comitative/abundant phrase 'with river(s) and wine' meaning there was abundance of flowing water and wine.
segments.s180-g92: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones / nobles'. به ایوان کاوس is a prepositional phrase 'to / at the iwan of Kay Kavus' (به here = 'at/in'). کی at the end functions as a temporal/relative 'when' linking this clause to what follows or to the previous action—a common poetic connective. Word order places subject + place phrase before the temporal connector.
segments.s180-g93: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign / lord of the world.' هشتم here is an ordinal adverbial ('on the eighth [day]') modifying the action; classical Persian often leaves the implied noun 'روز' (day) out. سر و تن is a coordinated object 'head and body.' بشست is past 3rd singular 'he washed' (poetic past form). The sentence uses poetic inversion: ordinal + subject + object + verb.
segments.s180-g94: بیاسود is a past 3rd singular (poetic b‑prefix + آسود) meaning 'he rested/was at ease' in context; here it introduces a following action. و simply coordinates the two verbs. جای نیایش is a noun phrase 'a place of prayer' (جای = place, نیایش = prayer/supplication). بجست is past 3rd singular 'he sought' (here: sought out/found). The two verbs are coordinated, giving successive actions: he rested and then sought a place to pray.
segments.s180-g95: به پیش = 'into the presence of / before.' خداوند is 'the Lord/God.' گردان سپهر is a poetic epithet: گردان 'revolving, circling' modifies سپهر 'the sky/heaven' — together 'the Lord of the revolving heavens' (an epithet of God). The whole phrase is a prepositional clause indicating destination: he goes before God.
segments.s180-g96: The clause is compact and uses poetic inversion. برفت is a past 3rd singular meaning 'he went/advanced' (here: he went forward into God's presence). آفرین را is the object phrase 'praise / an act of praise' (را marks it as definite/emphatic). بگسترد (past) 'he spread/unfolded' governs چهر 'his face' — idiomatically 'he turned/lifted his face in praise' (i.e. showed a face/expression of adoration). The word order places the object 'آفرین' before the verb for emphasis and then names the body part (چهر) affected.
segments.s180-g9: به + noun + شد is a poetic way to indicate setting out toward or entering an activity: به نخچیر شد = 'went to the hunt/entered into hunting.' شهریار جهان is a nominal phrase in apposition (شهریار 'king' qualified by جهان 'of the world'), placed after the verb phrase in this verse‑word order.
segments.s180-g10: ابا here functions as an archaic/poetic form meaning 'with' (classical variant of با) linking the king's action to his companion; رستم نامور پهلوان is a name followed by two epithets in apposition (رستم = proper name, نامور = 'renowned', پهلوان = 'hero/champion').
segments.s180-g11: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; برفتند is 3rd person plural past ('they departed/left'); آزادگان ('the freemen' or noble freemen) is the plural agent — poetic word order places the agent after the verb here.
segments.s180-g12: چو introduces examples ('such as' / 'like') and is repeated to list two persons (گیو و چو گودرز = 'Giv and Goudarz'); کشوادگان is an archaic/uncertain title or group-name attached to them and is treated as a noun label here (lexical sense unclear).
segments.s180-g13: که introduces a relative clause describing سپاهی ('the host/army'); شد is the copular/past 'became' and تیره is the adjective 'dark' applying to خورشید و ماه ('sun and moon') — a poetic image (the army so dire that the sun and moon are darkened). Word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s180-g14: همی + verb is the classical progressive/imperfective past marker (همی رفت = 'was going/went'); the repeated با introduces companions ('with ... and with ...'). یوز is a hunting animal (cheetah) and باز شاه should be read as 'the king's falcon' (باز 'hawk/falcon' with شاه as its possessor placed after it, a common classical ordering).
segments.s180-g15: همه modifies بوم ایران ('the whole land of Iran'); سراسر is an adverb 'throughout/wholly'; بگشت here is a poetic past verb meaning 'turned/was transformed/changed' (an older poetic form — lexical sense in the provided glosses is uncertain).
segments.s180-g16: به + noun expresses motion/transition 'into' (به آباد و ویرانی = 'into prosperity and ruin'); اندر is another locative/prepositional particle ('into/within') and گذشت is the past 'passed' — together they form 'passed into/underwent prosperity and destruction.'
segments.s180-g57: This clause orders elements as temporal adverb + oblique object + noun + verb + numeral: کنون (now) sets time; از تو سوگند is an oblique object meaning “an oath from you”; خواهم is first‑person present/future ('I will [ask/take]'); یکی after the verb/verb‑phrase specifies ‘one (oath)’. The whole is equivalent to “I will take one oath from you.”
segments.s180-g58: نباید که is a negative modal construction meaning 'it must not be that' / 'you must not'. پیچی is a present/subjunctive second‑person form of پیچیدن (here without an initial ب‑, as is common in older/poetic Persian) meaning 'you turn/swerve'. ز داد اندکی is a prepositional phrase 'even a little from justice' with ز marking separation/source; اندکی ('a little') modifies the degree of turning.
segments.s180-g59: که introduces a subordinate/content clause 'that ...'. The proper name پرکین is the affected person; کنی is second‑person present/subjunctive of کردن meaning 'you cause/make' (here 'cause/turn'); دل ز افراسیاب literally 'the heart from Afrasiab' — object marked by ز meaning 'away from Afrasiab'. Word order places the person being acted on (پرکین) before the verb.
segments.s180-g60: دمی is an adverbial 'for a moment/instant' placed up front. آتش اندر نیاری به آب is a compact clause meaning roughly 'you would not bring/put fire into water' — here expressing the idea of quenching or applying water to fire; اندر is a locative 'in/into'. The lexical form نیاری in the edition's lemma list doesn't match usual senses, so its exact morphological status is archaic/poetic (negative or subjunctive shape), but syntactically it functions as the verb of the clause. به آب 'into/to water' marks the target.
segments.s180-g61: ز خویشی مادر is a prepositional phrase 'because of/from the mother's kinship' (ز marking source/reason). بدو is an allative pronoun 'to him' and نگروی is a negative/imperative/subjunctive‑shaped form meaning 'do not take/regard (as)' — here 'do not regard him as kin on the basis of his mother's kinship.' The verb is placed at the end as typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s180-g62: This clause contains two coordinated imperatives/negations: نپیچی is a negative command from پیچیدن ('do not turn/as you are not to swerve/turn aside'); و 'and' links the clauses; گفت here appears in a usage that the edition's lemma lists as a noun but in context functions as 'what is said' or a verbal notion, followed by کسی 'by/anyone' and نشمری 'do not count/consider' (negative imperative). The whole means 'and do not turn aside and do not regard what anyone says as counting/being important.'
segments.s180-g63: به گنج و فزونی is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by/with treasure and abundance'. نگیری is a negative imperative/subjunctive of گرفتن ('do not take'), and فریب is the noun 'deception/enticement' — together: 'do not be taken (deceived) by treasure and plenty.' The verb is in a prohibitive sense addressed to the addressee.
segments.s180-g64: همان‌گر begins a conditional/comparative clause 'if/just as (likewise)'; فراز is 'height/top/rising fortune' and نشیب 'descent/decline' are contrasted. آیدت‌گر is a fused conditional 'آید + ت + گر' meaning 'if it comes to you' (i.e. 'if you rise'). The clause is elliptical—the verb is attached to فراز and the contrast with نشیب is left without a repeated verb—yielding 'whether you are raised or lowered.'
